Question 3 (18 marks) 
A company manufactures fidget toys using two different machines. Machine A produces 1500 
parts per day and machine B produces 500 parts per day. To make a fidget toy, three parts from 
machine A and one part from machine B are needed. 
 
a. A sample of 80 parts made by Machine A is taken and found that 4 parts were defective. 
 
i. What is the point estimate of the proportion of defective parts produced by machine A? 
1 mark 
 
 
ii. Calculate the 96% confidence interval for parts made by machine A, correct to 4 decimal 
places? 
2 marks 
 
 
 
 
 
iii. What is the estimate for the actual number of defective parts produced by machine A? 
1 mark 
 
 
 
iv. If the cost to manufacture each part is $1, to cover the cost of defective part, how much 
extra should be added to the cost price (to the nearest cent), based on the 96% confidence 
interval? 
1 mark 
 
 
 
SECTION B – continued 
TURN OVER
2022 MATHEMATICAL METHODS EXAM 2 
 TSSM 2022                                                                                                                                                            Page 16 of 22 
b. The number of defective parts produced by machine B each day is normally distributed with a 
mean of 20. 
 
i. If on 77.34% of days the number of defective parts produced is 23 or less, show that the 
variance is 16. 
2 marks 
 
 
 
 
 
 
ii. Hence, on any given day, find the probability that the number of defective parts produced 
by machine B is less than 15, correct to 4 decimal places. 
1 mark 
 
 
 
c. One day, machine A produced 100 defective parts. Machine B produced 20 defective parts. 
Three parts from machine A were selected at random. One part from machine B was selected 
at random. 
 
Let A represent there being at least one of defective part in the sample from machine A and B 
represent the part from machine B being defective. 
 
i. Calculate the probability that all three parts from machine A are not defective, correct to 4 
decimal places. 
1 mark

Question 14 
The algorithm below, written in pseudocode, estimates the value of a definite integral using 
the trapezium rule. 
 
Inputs: 
f(x), the function to integrate 
 
 
a, the lower terminal of integration 
 
 
b, the upper terminal of integration 
 
 
n, the number of trapeziums to use 
 
 
 
Define  trap(f(x), a, b, n) 
      w ← (b - a) ÷ n 
      sum ← f(a) + f(b) 
      x ← a + w 
For i from 1 to n - 1  
 
 
sum ←  sum + 2 × f(x) 
 
 
x ←  x + w 
 
EndFor 
 
area ← (w ÷ 2) × sum 
 
Return area

10.5

15.3
